title: Performance
text: Performance The performance of an application becomes an issue if users find that the application speed (elapsed time between an end-user request and the application's response) does not meet expectations or if the application is unavailable when required. Performance depends on such factors as: Available computing resources System load System efficiency Concurrency Transaction complexity Rush hour processing In practice, there might be only a limited budget for optimizing performance, so it makes sense to first address those issues with the highest impact on performance. Performance Optimization Checklist The following checklist presents the areas of interest based on their impact on performance. The order is not absolute and you might want to reconsider it for your particular circumstances. For example, database indexes and layout of entities are related to data design, so you might consider them together. However, as a general rule, it is recommended that you examine the issues in the following order: Expectations regarding response times Available computing resources System load balancing—See Load Balancing Data design—See Data Design Process contention—See Performance Contention Component logic—See Performance Guidelines for Component Logic and Implementation Component implementation—See Component logic Database indexes Choice of components—See Component Implementations and Performance Swapping Web servers DBMS tuning—See Database Tuning Operating system and the network Layout of entities—See Layout of Entities and Fields Was this topic helpful?
